sections.What we found: Looking at these recorded values for a 79-year-old woman, a few things stand out. Your oxygen level dipped at its lowest to 89% (with a last reading of 93% and an average around 95%), which is a little below the usual comfortable range. Your diastolic (bottom number) blood pressure was recorded as low as 35, with a last reading also at 35, and your mean arterial pressure reached a low of 56 — both on the low side, though your systolic (top number) stayed in a reasonable range. On the labs, your potassium was 3.1, which is below the usual range; your hemoglobin was 10.9, suggesting mild anemia; and albumin (3.3), total bilirubin (1.5), and the liver enzymes AST (37) and ALT (39) were each just slightly outside typical ranges. Most other values — heart rate, temperature, sodium, glucose, kidney measures, white cells and platelets — look within normal bounds. No past conditions or prior admissions are on record.
sections.What may be contributing: Some of these numbers tend to travel together. The low diastolic and mean arterial pressures, taken alongside a slightly low albumin, can reflect fluid balance or circulation patterns worth a clinician's eye. The mildly low oxygen readings and the mild anemia can each affect how well oxygen is carried and delivered, so they're worth considering together. The slightly raised liver enzymes and bilirubin form a small cluster that a clinician may want to look at as a group rather than one at a time. These are observations from the recorded values, not proven causes.
sections.What this means: This is a plain-language reading of recorded numbers, not a diagnosis or a clinical assessment. None of these values on its own tells a complete story, and a single snapshot can be influenced by timing, measurement, and how you were feeling at the moment. Several findings are only mildly outside the usual range, so they should be read gently and in the context of how you actually feel. Because I'm working only from these numbers and not examining you, treat this as a prompt for conversation rather than a conclusion.
sections.What to do next: It would be worth sharing these results with your doctor, particularly the low potassium, the mild anemia, the low blood-pressure readings, and the slightly elevated liver values, so they can decide whether any repeat testing or follow-up makes sense. Bringing this summary to your next appointment can help you ask focused questions about which findings, if any, need monitoring over time.
sections.When to seek care urgently: Seek urgent medical help if you have trouble breathing or persistent shortness of breath, chest pain, fainting or severe dizziness, a very fast or irregular heartbeat, confusion, muscle weakness or cramps, yellowing of the skin or eyes, or if you simply feel suddenly and seriously unwell.

report.disclaimer: This summary is generated by a computer model to help you understand your health information. It is not a diagnosis and not a substitute for advice from a qualified clinician. Always talk to your healthcare provider about your results and any decisions.
